observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
a) instruct the MT to:
i. intensify collections by undertaking more effective revenue collection
strategies, such as periodic sending of the notices of delinquency and
tax bills either in person or via mail service, and coordination with
barangay officials and national and local offices that have access to
the addresses of delinquent taxpayers; and
ii. avail of all legal and administrative remedies allowed under R.A. No.
7160;
b) instruct the Municipal Accountant (MA) to:
i. prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the actual RPT and
SET Receivables using the amounts per Summary of RPT
Delinquencies from the MTO as of December 31, 2024, and set up at
the beginning of the succeeding year the amount based on the
Assessment Rolls provided by the OMA, in the absence of RPTAR, to
wit:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Deferred RPT ₱88,551,294.17
SET Receivable 543,816.58
RPT Receivable ₱88,551,294.17
Deferred SET 543,816.58
To correct the overstatement in Receivable and Deferred Income
accounts
ii. make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the improper
recording of discounts on advance payments of RPT and SET and
account misclassification errors, viz:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Other Deferred Credits ₱5,817,747.36
Discount on Advance Payments 654,496.58
Deferred RPT ₱1,454,436.85
Deferred SET 1,818,046.05
Due to LGUs 3,199,761.04
To correct the understatement in Deferred Charges and the related
Liability accounts
c) enjoin the MAO, the MTO, and the OMA to reconcile their respective
records on RPT to establish the actual RPT/SET Receivables accruing to
the Municipality;
d) direct the OIC-ME, as the Building Official, to inform all applicants for
building permits of their post-construction responsibilities, including the
mandatory filing of a sworn declaration of the true value of the structure
upon completion; and
e) require the OMA to declare all identified undeclared properties in the
name of the defaulting owner, if known, or under an “unknown owner”
classification, as the case may be, and assess them for taxation in
accordance with existing laws, rules, and regulations.

We recommended that management:
a. strictly enforce the observance of fidelity bond limits by ensuring that no CA
shall be granted to any SDO beyond the allowable maximum cash
accountability covered by their approved bond;
b. in cases where increased accountability is necessary due to the volume or
urgency of programs, management should require the concerned SDOs to
apply for an increase in bond coverage; and
c. instruct the MA to refrain from allowing additional CAs to any official or
employee unless the previous cash advance given is first settled or a proper
accounting thereof is made and issue a certification that the previous cash
advance of the concerned accountable officer was already liquidated.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the (a) Storekeeper to (i) conduct a
periodic physical inventory count of supplies and materials; and (ii) prepare and
submit the RPCI to the AT not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year; (iii)
maintain a complete and regularly updated stock cards for each inventory item; and
(b) MA to (i) promptly derecognize in the accounting records all items that have been
consumed or distributed to end-users in a timely manner; and (iii) record all
procurement of inventory supplies using the Perpetual Inventory System to ensure
real-time monitoring and accurate reporting of inventory balances.

We recommended that Management:
a) advise the MA to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the delayed
recognition of depreciation on Infrastructure Assets, and Buildings and Other
Structures, caused by the late transfer from CIP to their appropriate accounts;
b) instruct the Inventory Committee to perform a comprehensive physical
inventory of all PPE and ensure that the results are properly documented in
the required RPCPPE, in accordance with applicable regulations;
c) direct the MA, MT, and Storekeeper to ensure that the PPE records are
updated in a timely manner, including PCs and PPELCs to ensure proper
control, monitoring, and accountability over the agency’s property and
equipment;
d) undertake the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to improve the
reliability of the PPE accounts in both accounting records and property
records, thereby facilitating more effective reconciliation between the two
offices.;
e) instruct the MT to prioritize the registration of all motor vehicles owned by
the Municipality with the LTO to prevent possible loss and misuse of the assets
and to avoid incurring penalties, fines or sanctions from the LTO; (ii) facilitate
the insurance of all motor vehicles owned to ensure the LGU is indemnified or
compensated for any damage or loss due to possible casualties that may
happen on these insurable assets; and
f) consider hiring a qualified GSO who will be responsible for overseeing the
effective management, safekeeping, and maintenance of all PPE.

We recommended that the LCE:
a) instruct the MA to:
i. draw the following accounting entries to correct the misstatements in
Leave Benefits Payable and Government Equity accounts:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Government Equity ₱2,711,438.76
Leave Benefits Payable ₱2,711,438.76
ii. reclassify the non-current portion of the Leave Benefits Payable for
the proper presentation of the account in the Statement of Financial
Position;
b) direct the HRMO to:
i. ensure the timely and accurate submission of the Schedule of the
Monetary Value of Accrued Leave Credits to the MAO for proper
recording of the estimated cost of accumulating paid absences;
ii. submit the 36 unsubmitted leave cards to the AT for further
evaluation;
iii. initiate and prioritize the reconciliation and correction of balances in
the leave cards; and
iv. revisit its process for updating and maintaining leave cards.
Specifically, the revised process should include regularly updating
leave cards and other internal control measures to provide timely and
accurate updates;
c) enjoin the MA and the HRMO to institute a periodic audit of all employees’
accumulated leaves before approval and payment of their respective
monetized leave credits and terminal leave benefits; and
d) set aside additional reserve funds for the accumulated compensated leave
credits as of year-end; henceforth, ensure the adequacy of the funds provided
for the leave credits earned thereafter by including the said benefits in the
annual budget.

We recommended that Management instruct the MA and the HRMO to
continuously perform reconciliation between their records and take the necessary
actions to address the unremitted balance caused by inconsistent recording of
employees’ salaries, which resulted in a system error in the GSIS.

We recommended that Management:
a) instruct the BAC to (i) adopt well-defined strategies to strengthen
monitoring and evaluation during the implementation phase, thereby
ensuring the timely execution of PPAs, and enabling prompt corrective
actions on identified gaps; (ii) specify implementation timelines for
development PPAs in the SAPP; and (iii) henceforth, align procurement
schedules with the specified timelines indicated in the SAPP;
b) require the winning contractor to submit the DOLE-approved CSHP
within the prescribed period; and
c) ensure the timely posting of the NOA and NTP on PhilGEPS within the
prescribed period.

We recommended that the LCE to:
a) instruct the MA and the MBO to reconcile the Report of LDRRMF
Utilization and the Registry of Appropriation to determine the huge
discrepancy of reported available balance and utilization of continuing
appropriation amounting to ₱7,080,567.46;
b) remind the MA to observe the rules of transferring the/unobligated
balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE in the Trust Liability-
DRRM account; and
c) direct the LDRRM Officer to (i) include in the preparation of the
LDRRMFIP/AIP the list of PPAs to be undertaken out of the unexpended
balance of the prior year LDRRMF to rationalize the use of the STF; and
(ii) strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs
to achieve the goals and objectives of the fund and the desired benefits.

We recommended that Management (a) establish and implement clear and
time-bound turnover protocols and procedures to ensure the prompt transfer of
completed projects to intended end-users; and (b) direct the OIC-ME to coordinate
with the contractors to facilitate the regular updating of the project status in
compliance with the guidelines on information and publicity for PPAs.

We recommended that the LSB:
a) furnished a copy of the SEF Budget to the MA in a timely manner to
facilitate proper recording and monitoring of SEF transactions;
b) ensure that the date of transmittal of the SEF Budget to the concerned
departments is clearly indicated on the receiving copy maintained on file;
and
c) submit the required quarterly and annual reports on SEF utilization using
the SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 (Annex B), as prepared by the MA,
to the DepEd CO through appropriate channels, with copies furnished to the
local sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG.

We recommended that the LCE undertake the following courses of action:
a) instruct the LGU GFPS to monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs to
ensure that the same are implemented as planned to attain GAD’s goals
and objectives;
b) issue an appropriate policy directive, copy furnished to the DILG Office
that conducted the review of the Municipality’s GPB, to ensure the
dissemination and implementation of the GPB; and shall mandate the
Municipal GFPS to: i) provide technical assistance in the implementation
of the GPB; ii) monitor its implementation; iii) ensure the preparation and
submission of GAD AR; and iv) consolidate reports on the implementation
of the GPB; and
c) instruct the GFP to submit the documentary requirements supporting the
GAD AR.

We commended the Management for its substantial compliance with the
established headcount requirements for casual and JO personnel, budget
appropriation for PS within limitation set by R.A. No. 7160, and for maintaining
complete documentary requirements for claims of salaries and wages of casual and
JO personnel. However, we recommended that Management re-evaluate the current
personnel complement to fill-up vacancies.

We recommended that Management enforce immediate compliance on the
audit suspensions and disallowances as prescribed by Section 7.1.1 of 2009 RRSA.
However, we recommended that the MAO ensure that the original copies of DVs bearing complete and important details are submitted to the AT within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. ensure that all funds received from SAs are supported with duly signed MOA/TA to clearly define the specific guidelines and procedures in the implementation of individual projects, including the responsibilities of the Municipality, thereby ensuring timely accomplishment thereof for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries;
b. instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), the ME, and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to (i) indicate in the SPP the specific timelines of implementation of development PPAs; henceforth, (ii) align the timing of procurement processes with the specific timelines set in the SPP; (iii) prepare the plans and programs taking into account the provisions of the RIRR of R.A. No. 9184 on the conduct of pre-engineering study before undergoing the detailed engineering to ensure the viability of the proposed projects, thereby preventing constant revision of the approved plans and programs; and (iv) adopt well-defined strategies to enhance monitoring and program evaluation during the implementation and implementation-ready and implementation-phase, ensuring the timely implementation of PPAs, and so that immediate appropriate and corrective actions could be done on noted gaps; and
c. comply with the necessary posting requirements in the utilization of NTF-ELCAC Funds and the subsequent notification thereof to the oversight agencies for transparency and information purposes.
observations.managementAction: The necessity of MOA is not required during the year, as the Municipality possesses the capacity to implement the project based on LBC No. 154 dated February 6, 2024. The Municipality prepared a Comprehensive Program of Work, Detailed Estimates, and a Detailed Engineering Plan before the execution of procurement activities. A fund transfer amounting to ₱2,500,000.00 was executed from the GF to the TF as evidenced by the Authority for Fund Transfer dated June 26, 2024 and confirmation letter dated June 26, 2024 sent to DILG-RO. The BAC Secretariat will monitor the issuance of NOA and NTP to ensure compliance with the required posting period on PhilGEPS.

The ME submitted an explanation for the non-implementation of the development projects and attached the resolutions for the reprogrammed projects. The Municipality ensures that surety bonds posted by contractors specify the exact coverage period. Certificates of Final Acceptance are only issued after the defects liability period. The MAO ensures strict scrutiny of the validity period of performance and warranty securities before payment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the MA and the MT to cause the immediate transfer of cash to the LDRRMF bank account at year-end or at the earliest possible time to reflect the correct individual balance of Cash in Bank accounts in the FS and to ensure that proper cash back up was established for DRRM PPAs in the STF, and henceforth enhancing transparency and accountability in the usage of the same; and b. direct the MA to record the acquisition/construction of capital assets out of the STF using the prescribed account therein.
observations.managementAction: The MA submitted JEV No. 401-2024-01-05-003 dated January 31, 2024 which effected fund transfer. The MA furnished to the AT copies of JEV No. 401-2024-01-05-007 dated April 19, 2024 recording the acquisition/construction of capital assets charged to STF.

The Municipality submitted the pertinent supporting documents. The LDRRMFIP was updated. Copies of the relevant supporting documents were submitted to the AT. The LDRRM Officer included the necessary PPAs to be funded from the unexpended balances and the Municipal Office monitors and evaluates the utilization of the LDRRMF.

The LGU SWM SCMAR for the 1st semester of CY 2024 was submitted within the prescribed period. The Municipality prepared a Purchase Request for suitable PPE for garbage collectors. The OIC-MENRO coordinated with the concerned Barangays for the operations of EMB-funded Barangay MRFs. However, the Deed of Donations for the MRFs and the whereabouts of the unaccounted waste shredder machine are still being processed. The Municipality scheduled the installation of three-phase transformer electrical system in the MSL.

The MT assigned a competent personnel and coordinated with the BPLO. Notification letters were issued to taxpayers with unrenewed business permits to settle their accounts. The Municipality advised the SB to institutionalize the use of PILAA. The LCE instructed the designated BPLO to require taxpayers to submit complete documentary requirements. Several suppliers were issued notifications to settle their delinquent business taxes.

For CY 2024, the Municipality registered a high utilization rate of 89.97% and enacted Ordinance No. 213-24 and Executive Order No. 18 Series of 2024 reorganizing the GFPS. The Municipality created a GAD Database and submitted the annual GAD AR. The LCE instructed the designated BPLO/GFPS to require the submission of supporting documents. Trainings on HGDG are being planned and some programs were mainstreamed to other program funds.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

draft/make clear guidelines on the turnover of completed projects between the Municipality and its intended beneficiaries/
barangays to avoid confusion and/or delay in the use of the projects; 

install feedback mechanism and risk assessment of agency operations to have a third-party assessment of the completed project;
 
review the policies and procedures for the monitoring and reporting of the utilization of 20% DF to ensure that the completed projects were serving their purpose for the benefit of the general public; and

direct the ME to coordinate with the contractors to comply with the required posting of information and publicity on programs/projects/
activities of government agencies and to inform the AT within ten (10) days after the award of the project or before the start of the program/project/

observations.managementAction:

Appropriate feedback mechanisms were installed during the year.



Appropriate measures were installed to ensure that completed projects were serving their intended purpose.
observations.reason: Our ocular inspection of signboards for infrastructure projects charged against the 20% DF revealed that relevant information such as project duration, the target date of completion, percentage of completion to date, cost incurred to date, and the actual date of completion were not updated and filled out. Furthermore, no appropriate notifications were received by the AT relative to the posting of signboards at the project sites.

No clear-cut policy guidelines on the engagement of services of the casual and JO personnel of the Municipality were devised during the year.

Validation made by the AT during the year revealed that the hiring of JO/casual personnel was still to augment the Municipality’s regular workforce and not for the implementation of the local projects.

In various instances during the year, regular employees have more time to engage in other activities and became dependent on the outputs of the JOs/casuals, rather than performing their jobs religiously.

Some plantilla vacant positions were not filled up during the year.

Some ARs were still expressed in generic terms and not for day-to-day work details.

observations.recommendation:

instruct the MPDC to facilitate the gathering of data/information needed for the creation of an updated and well-established GAD database to have an accurate and reliable basis in the planning and programming of PPAs as well as in the policy formulation to properly address the existing and emerging GAD issues and concerns; 

direct the GFPS to fully implement the GAD PPAs and monitor their implementation to ensure that optimum benefits are derived by its constituents; and

direct the GFPS and GAD key personnel to (i) undergo capacity-building programs and training on GAD to enhance their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools, among others; and

unexpended balances of the LDRRMF into the LDRRMFIP to rationalize
the use of the STF;
c) specify clearly in the LDRRMFIP the schedule or period of project
implementation to avoid delay in the project execution;
d) incorporate GAD-related PPAs in the LDRRMFIP to ensure that disaster
risk reduction measures are gender-responsive;
e) monitor and evaluate the utilization of the LDRRMF based on the PPAs
listed in the LDRRMFIP to ensure the full implementation thereof; and
f) regularly review unimplemented PPAs and ascertain the viability of
implementation therefrom, otherwise, realign or reprogram for more
viable and significant PPAs to maximize the utilization of LDRRMF.

11.38 We recommended that Management:
a) require the MENRO to prepare the LGU SWM SCMAR and submit the
same within the prescribed period to the Regional Office No. III and
SWMD-PDTSS to monitor promptly the status of compliance with R.A.
No. 9003;
b) abide with the project schedule to ensure the full implementation of all the
SWM priority programs targeted during the year for the benefit of the
Municipality and the constituents in general;
c) ensure that garbage collectors are equipped with suitable personal
protective equipment or gear to free them from the hazards of handling
solid wastes;
d) instruct the OIC-MENRO in close coordination with the MEO and other
concerned departments to: (i) strictly monitor and provide technical
assistance to the concerned Barangays to facilitate the operations of EMB-
funded Barangay MRFs for an efficient and effective collection and
disposal of solid wastes, and as an additional measure to mitigate health
and environmental risks. If warranted, initiate necessary steps to process
the formal turnover of completed MRFs including the machines located
within the territorial jurisdiction of the concerned Barangays to transfer
the responsibility in managing and maintaining the properties; (ii) submit
a written explanation on the whereabouts of the unaccounted waste
shredder machine noted during the ocular inspection for proper
disposition by the AT; and (iii) prioritize the installation of three-phase
transformer electrical system in the MSL to maximize the use of waste
pulverizer and shredder machines and to avoid further deterioration of the
same.

13.30 We recommended that Management instruct the GFPS to:
a) monitor the implementation of organization-focused GAD PPAs to ensure
that the same are implemented as planned to attain GAD’s goals and
objectives;
b) attach the documents required under Paragraph 5 of Section 4.0 (C.8) of
JMC 2013-01 to facilitate the monitoring and evaluation of the GAD PPAs
implemented during the year;
c) observe the deadline for submission of GAD AR to the AT;
d) create a centralized GAD Database that would consolidate the information
gathered and compiled by all offices to include gender statistics and other
relevant sex-disaggregated data as a basis in GAD planning, programming,
and policy-making;
e) administer the HGDG test whenever it uses attribution in the budgeting
phase and AR preparation; and
f) seek help from experts and include HGDG training in the budget to ensure
the correctness of the GAD issues identified and the responsiveness of the
corresponding GAD PPAs.

14.10 We recommended that Management:
a) instruct the MA and the MT including all the AOs to (i) immediately
transmit to the AT the unsubmitted accounts and financial reports; and
(ii) strictly observe the timeframes on the submission of accounts and
financial reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006; and
b) immediately require the concerned personnel to cooperate in the
preparation and submission of AAPSI for the AT to monitor the status of
implementation of audit recommendations and validate the actions taken,
if any.

15.6 We commended the Management particularly the MAO and the MTO for the
prompt remittance of the taxes withheld during the current year to the BIR. This
notwithstanding, we recommended that Management continuously take
responsibility to comply with the withholding and timely remittance of taxes in
conformity with RR No. 2-98 and RMC No. 23-2007 on the prompt remittance of the
taxes due to the BIR.

16.6 We commended the Management for complying with the provisions of R.A.
No. 9679 and its IRR on the proper deduction of Pag-IBIG contributions of its
personnel and the remittance thereof within the prescribed period and recommended
the continuous observance of the requirements of the Act.

17.5 We commended the Management for complying with the provisions of the
Revised IRR of the National Health Insurance Act of 2013 on the proper deduction
of PhilHealth contributions of its personnel and the remittance thereof within the
prescribed period and recommended the continuous observance thereof for the
benefit of its employees.

19.4 We recommended that Management enforce immediate compliance on the
audit suspensions and disallowances as prescribed by Section 7.1.1 of 2009 RRSA.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a) instruct the MT to (i) intensify collections by undertaking effective
revenue collection strategies such as periodic sending of the notice of
delinquencies and tax bills by personal or mail service and coordination
with barangay officials and national and local offices that have access to
the addresses of delinquent taxpayers; (ii) avail of all legal and
administrative remedies allowed under R.A. No. 7160; (iii) submit the
RPTAR/certified list of taxpayers and the amount of taxes due and
submit a copy of the same to the Municipal Accountant; and (v) maintain
a close coordination with the Municipal Assessor as regards the newly
assessed real properties; and
b) require the Municipal Accountant to secure the RPTAR/Taxpayer’s
Index Card from the MT in order to come up with the basis for accurate
and reliable amounts of RPT/SET Receivables.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to draw JEVs taking up the (i) record under the
appropriate CIP accounts based on actual completion of projects as shown
in the SWA from the contractors validated by the Municipal Engineer at
year-end; (ii) prepare adjusting entries to record the accumulated
depreciation for the finished projects and (iii) continue to update the
PPELC for all property accounts to support the recorded balances of PPE;
b. Property Custodian/Municipal Treasurer to (i) maintain complete and
updated property cards per category of PPE; and (ii) report all obsolete
and unserviceable PPE in the Inventory and Inspection Report on
Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) duly signed by the Head of Accounting
Unit, Inspector/Appraisal Committee and approved by Head of Agency or
authorized representative, and submit the report to the Disposal
Committee for proper disposition;
c. Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) to recognize the obligation of the
construction of infrastructure projects based on progress billings and
accomplishment reports submitted by the contractors;
d. Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a complete physical count of PPE; and
(ii) prepare/submit the corresponding RPCPPE (with appropriate
remarks on the condition and whereabouts of the PPE) to the Accounting,
GSO and COA; and
e. Concerned officials and committees to conduct the one-time cleansing of
PPE Accounts Balances of the Municipality in compliance with COA
Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to (i)
revert to the GF of the long-outstanding and invalid claims recorded in the books of
the Municipality; (ii) to confirm from the concerned creditors the said payables and
require them to submit documents pertaining to their claims; and (iii) in case the
creditors fail to support their claims, revert the amount to the Unappropriated
Surplus of the GF upon approval of the Sangguniang Bayan.

We recommended that Management instruct the:
a. LHB to submit a written explanation on the non-certification of the list of
the public HCWs and non-HCWs who were eligible to receive OCA;
b. concerned official to submit an explanation in the internal arrangement of
sharing the OCA to HCWs and Non-HCWs who were not on the official
list submitted to DOH and the reason for not including the said personnel
to the list submitted if they were indeed eligible to receive the benefits.
c. concerned officer/employees provide valid proof of the discrepancies noted
in the risk classifications of some of the HCWs and Non-HCWs;
d. HCWs and Non-HCWs concerned to provide valid proofs that they
physically reported for work and have provided direct health/support care
as well as the actual and detailed accomplishment reports relative thereto;
and
e. MHO or concerned supervisor, being the head of the health facility, to
ensure that Accomplishment Reports involved COVID-19 response in line
with the prevention, detection, isolation, treatment, rehabilitation and
vaccination strategy.

We recommended that Management:
a. draft/make a clear guideline on the turnover of completed projects between
the Municipality and its intended beneficiary/barangays to avoid confusion
and/or delay in the use of the projects;
b. install feedback mechanism and risk assessment of agency operations to
have third-party assessment of the completed project;
c. review the policies and procedures for the monitoring and reporting of the
utilization of 20% Development Fund to ensure that the completed projects
were servings their purpose for the benefit of the general public; and
d. direct the Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the contractors to comply
with the required posting of information and publicity on
programs/projects/activities of government agencies and to inform the
audit team within ten (10) days after the award of the project or before the
start of the program/project/activity that the appropriate project
signboards and/or public notices are already posted at the project site as
stated in COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

We recommended that LCE in coordination with the HRMO (a) prepare and
issue policy guidelines to address the issues on the necessity and propriety of hiring
casuals and job orders in order to limit expenditures to programs/projects where
their services are actually needed; (b) hire job order personnel based on necessity and
skills/competencies specifically required in the local projects; (c) avoid the hiring of
job orders whose functions overlap or duplicate those of the regular employees of the
Municipality; (d) re-evaluate the personnel complements to fill-up the vacancies; and
(e) ensure that all personnel complies with the required biometric scanning and that
the accomplishment reports were detailed to reflect the day-to-day accomplishments
of the employees.

We recommended that Management require the MDRRMC to:
a. ensure that the LDRRMF is optimally utilized;
b. refund the LDRRMF from the GF for the amount used to purchase the fire
alarm;
c. properly plan for the inclusion/integration of activities to be funded from
the STF of the LDRRMF into the LDRRMFIP based on an extensive
evaluation of the vulnerability of the Municipality to natural or man-made
disaster risks to rationalize the use of the STF; and
d. sustain the current effective responses to disasters and its attendant risks.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant (a) trace, analyze and reconcile
the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and make the necessary
adjustments to reflect the balances of the said accounts and furnish the Audit Team
the corrections made; and (b) prepare and maintain the necessary subsidiary records
supporting the balances of the said reciprocal accounts.

We recommended that Management undertake the following courses of
action:
a. instruct the MPDC to facilitate the gathering of data/information needed
for the creation of an updated and well-established GAD database to have
an accurate and reliable basis in the planning and programming of PPAs
as well as in the policy formulation in order to properly address the existing
and emerging GAD issues and concerns;
b. direct the GFPS to fully implement the GAD PPAs and monitor their
implementation to ensure that optimum benefits are derived by its
constituents; and
c. direct the GFPS and GAD key personnel to (i) undergo capacity building
programs and training on gender and development to enhance their
knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and
international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis,
gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools, among
others; and (ii) facilitate the establishment of the GAD RC to help and
assist the concerned officials in the proper monitoring of the
implementation of GAD related expenses and the evaluation of the
effectiveness of the same.

We commended management for making an effort on the timely submission
of accounts for 11 instances noted and showed improvement to prevent delays in the
rendition of accounts. However, we still recommend to continue in strictly adhering
to the prescribed timeframes on the rendition of accounts and
preparation/submission of financial reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that Management enforce immediate compliance on the
audit suspensions and disallowances as prescribed by Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular
No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Local Chief Executive (LCE) to undertake the following courses of action:
instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to determine the appraised value of PPEs for presentation of fair and true value of the asset accounts and provide for impairment losses, if warranted; (ii) disclose in the Notes to FS the  carrying amount of  property, plant and equipment retired from active use and held for disposal so as not to mislead users of the Financial Statements; and (iii) upon disposal, draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to derecognize the unserviceable properties from the  Accounting books  and  Property Records;
direct the Storekeeper to (i) maintain complete and updated property cards per category of PPE; and (ii) report all obsolete and unserviceable PPE in the IIRUP duly signed by the Head of Accounting Unit, Inspector/Appraisal Committee and approved by Head of Agency or authorized representative, and submit the report to the Disposal Committee for proper disposition; and
require the Inventory Committee to prepare/submit the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) (with appropriate remarks on the condition and whereabouts of the PPE) to the Accounting, GSO and COA.

We recommended that LCE to direct/instruct:
the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) transfer the financial assistance from various NGAs from the GF and SEF to the TF books in accordance with existing accounting policy; (ii) trace the records/documents relative to the unaccounted balances, reconcile the same, and remit the correct balance to the source agencies; (iii) coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the return of unused funds to the SAs or to the National Treasury, as the case may be; and
the Municipal Treasurer to draw a check for the remittance of unutilized amount of all completed projects including trust collections for ante-mortem and slaughterhouse fees after proper reconciliation/analysis done by the Accounting Office.

instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare the aforementioned adjusting/correcting entries; (ii) analyse the appropriate accounting entries when taking up grants from NGAs with requirement of liquidations (iii) submit the deficient documents mentioned in this AOM and (iv) ensure proper documentations to support the claims;
require the BAC to review the mode of procurement in the supplemental APP before the implementation of PPAs; and
prior to liquidation/return of any unexpended balance of fund transfers from NGAs, communicate/clarify, as the circumstances may warrant, to the concerned NGAs, any misinterpretation or deviation from prescribed guidelines to maximize the use of the said funds.

instruct the MSWDO, in close coordination with the Barangay officials concerned and the DSWD, to organize a validation team who will conduct verification on the eligibility of the aforementioned cases based on the filled-up SAC forms; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed to direct/instruct:
the Municipal Accountant to (i) transfer the financial assistance from various NGAs from the General Fund and Special Education Fund to the Trust Fund books in accordance with existing accounting policy; (ii) trace the records/documents relative to the unaccounted balances, reconcile the same, and remit the correct balance to the source agencies; (iii) coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the return of unused funds to the source agencies (SAs) or to the National Treasury, as the case may be; (iv) draw adjusting/correcting entries for the procurement of various assets; and (v) scrutinize the completeness of supporting documents of claims before releasing the same for payment and prior to the recording of the transactions in the books of accounts;
the Municipal Treasurer to draw a check for the remittance of unutilized amount of all completed projects including trust collections for ante-mortem and slaughterhouse fees after proper reconciliation/analysis done by the Accounting Office; and
the BAC and TWG to adhere to the documentary requirements in all stages of the bidding processes and submit to the Audit Team the required documents for infrastructure projects implemented in CY 2018.

We recommended and management agreed to:
direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately transmit the unsubmitted monthly accounts and financial reports; and
strictly adhere to the prescribed timeframes on the rendition of accounts and preparation/submittal of financial reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Otherwise, we shall be constrained to enforce the provision of Section 122 of P.D. No. 1445 on the withholding of salaries of all Municipal Officials deemed responsible for late/non-reporting of agency accounts.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations and management agreed to:
Abide strictly with the project schedule to ensure the full implementation of all the priority projects targeted for the year for the benefit of the Municipality and the constituents in general;
Consider the equitable distribution of PPAs listed among the three important sectors of development as prescribed under DBM-DILG JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 and previous issuances, and as mandated by Sections 16 and 17 of the Local Government Code (LGC) of 1991, to be able to cater not only the infrastructure needs of the constituents but also other concerns such as environment, agriculture, fishery and livelihood programs, projects and activities which will directly address the actual needs of the intended beneficiaries; and
Adopt some well-defined strategies in order to enhance the present monitoring and review activities during the implementation and program evaluation phase, in order for the planners, implementers, as well as evaluators to continuously monitor the timely implementation of PPAs, and so that immediate appropriate and corrective actions could be done on noted gaps.

We recommended and management agreed to comply strictly with COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 and require the Municipal Engineering Office and other officials concerned to disclose all relevant information of PPAs for transparency and accountability.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Instruct the MENRO to (i) comply with the requirements of the law on waste segregation by placing trash bins with proper labels and by enforcing strictly the “No Segregation, No Collection Policy”; (ii) relocate the plastic shredder abandoned at the defunct MRF to a suitable location to avoid further damage on the equipment or return the same to the owner in case the Municipality has decided not to keep it; and (iii) monitor closely the operations of MRFs in 13 component barangays to ensure full compliance with the basic requirements of R.A. No. 9003;
Take appropriate action to expedite the necessary improvement on the Material Recovery Facility (MRF) of the Municipality to make it operational; and
Pursue the establishment of a functional Sanitary Landfill.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to fulfill faithfully their obligation to remit the barangay shares from RPT collections within five days after the end of each quarter in accordance with Section 36 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and Section 271 (d) of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended and management agreed to stop the practice of designating non-permanent employee to a position exercising power or capacity to control certain operations and those involving signing/approving of official documents that are best suited to a permanent or regular employee.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation and the Local Chief Executive agreed through the MDRRMO to ensure that the execution of projects designed to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disasters, such as those projects pertaining to disaster prevention/mitigation and disaster preparedness, be given utmost priority.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the LDRRM Officer to:
Ensure that the LDRRMF is used only for activities related to disaster risk management activities pursuant to existing laws, rules, and regulations; and
Adhere strictly to the government’s policy on protecting and conserving government funds and property by preventing excessive expenditures or uses of such funds and property.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to undertake the following courses of action:
Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to post the Invitation to Bid and other Notices in the PhilGEPS, in the Agency website, and in at least three conspicuous places in the Municipality to promote transparency and competitiveness in all aspects of procurement;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to scrutinize the completeness of supporting documents of claims before releasing the same for payment; and
Direct the BAC-TWG to invite representatives from COA and other observers in various stages of the bidding in accordance with provisions of Section 13 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended and management agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to (a) consolidate requisitions of similar nature and those received at about the same time to be able to plan the required mode of procurement; and (b) ensure that all procurements are done through competitive public bidding unless all the conditions set forth for other modes of procurement under Sections 48 to 53 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 are satisfactorily met to promote transparency, economy and efficiency in operations.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all persons responsible and liable to settle immediately the outstanding audit suspensions and disallowances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the management:	
	
Instruct the Accounting Department to draw a correcting entry in their books by recognizing Loan Receivables- Others for transactions erroneously debited to Due to GOCCs in order to reflect the actual amount of receivables from debtors; 

Require the concerned approving official to strictly impose the policy of requiring the presence of co-makers, collateral and notarized contracts to ensure enforceability of debt collection;


Issue demand letter to defaulting debtors; exert effort to collect settlement of all overdue loans; and whenever necessary and warranted, request assistance from government lawyers to enforce collection of the outstanding loans receivables with corresponding interest and penalty or to effect the provision stipulated in the contract on the garnishment of properties; and

File a formal request to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or Supervising Auditor (SA) for the write-off of accounts receivable which are dormant supported by the following documents:
Schedule of dormant accounts by accountable officer/debtor/government entity and by account, certified by the accountant and approved by the Head of the government entity;
Certified relevant documents validating the existence of the conditions, as applicable, such as:
Death Certificate issued by Philippine Statistics Authority (formerly National Statistics Office)
Proof of Insolvency
Certification from the Department of Trade and Industry that the debtor has no registered business
Certification from the Securities and Exchange Commission that the Corporation is no longer active
Certificate of no residence in the barangay of the municipality/city of last known address
Proof of exhaustion of all remedies to collect the receivables and demand to liquidate the cash advances and fund transfers, such as but not limited to copies of served or returned demand letters
Certification by Legal Officer of the entity of no pending case relative to the account
Certification by the responsible officials of the entity to the effect that there are no records/documents available to validate claim
Other justifications, like in the case of request for write-off due to loss of documents, the circumstances of the loss should be stated in the letter-request
In case of fund transfer, the unliquidated amount after reconciliation shall be supported by certification by the Chief Accountants and approved by the Heads of the source and implementing entities that the fund was utilized for the purpose, and certification from the recipient that the project was partially or fully implemented, supported by pictures of the implemented projects. (COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016)

Considerable delays ranging from fourteen days to three months were noted in the submission of accounts and financial reports for the months of January to October 2016 due to lack of manpower.

Reiterated under Observation No.5, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
2.8	We recommended that the management:
Instruct the Accounting Department to draw a correcting entry in their books by recognizing Loan Receivables- Others for transactions erroneously debited to Due to GOCCs in order to reflect the actual amount of receivables from debtors;
Require the concerned approving official to strictly impose the policy of requiring the presence of co-makers, collateral and notarized contracts to ensure enforceability of debt collection;
Issue demand letter to defaulting debtors; exert effort to collect settlement of all overdue loans; and whenever necessary and warranted, request assistance from government lawyers to enforce collection of the outstanding loans receivables with corresponding interest and penalty or to effect the provision stipulated in the contract on the garnishment of properties; and
File a formal request to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or Supervising Auditor (SA) for the write-off of accounts receivable which are dormant supported by the following documents:
Schedule of dormant accounts by accountable officer/debtor/government entity and by account, certified by the accountant and approved by the Head of the government entity;
Certified relevant documents validating the existence of the conditions, as applicable, such as:
Death Certificate issued by Philippine Statistics Authority (formerly National Statistics Office)
Proof of Insolvency
Certification from the Department of Trade and Industry that the debtor has no registered business
Certification from the Securities and Exchange Commission that the Corporation is no longer active
Certificate of no residence in the barangay of the municipality/city of last known address
Proof of exhaustion of all remedies to collect the receivables and demand to liquidate the cash advances and fund transfers, such as but not limited to copies of served or returned demand letters
Certification by Legal Officer of the entity of no pending case relative to the account
Certification by the responsible officials of the entity to the effect that there are no records/documents available to validate claim
Other justifications, like in the case of request for write-off due to loss of documents, the circumstances of the loss should be stated in the letter-request
In case of fund transfer, the unliquidated amount after reconciliation shall be supported by certification by the Chief Accountants and approved by the Heads of the source and implementing entities that the fund was utilized for the purpose, and certification from the recipient that the project was partially or fully implemented, supported by pictures of the implemented projects. (COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016)

5.7	We recommended that the management direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal Budget Officer to immediately transmit the unsubmitted accounts and financial reports for November and December 2016 and henceforth adhere strictly to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts and preparation/submittal of financial reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

6.12	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the various appointive officials to discontinue the payment of membership dues in favor of their respective leagues/associations and the charging of meals for their monthly meetings; otherwise, succeeding payments will be disallowed in audit.

7.13	We recommended that management (a) develop a standard and set of guidelines in the granting of financial assistance and donations to serve as basis on the implementation of the LGU’s various assistance programs and to ensure that the qualifications of beneficiaries, purposes of assistance, amounts granted, and documentary requirements are well defined for purposes of promoting fairness and equity.  Money belonging to the public cannot be used for private ends; and (b) direct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all claims of financial assistance are duly supported with complete documentation.

9.7	We reiterated our previous recommendation that management (a) institutionalize a working and functional GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) or a similar GAD mechanism to catalyze and accelerate gender mainstreaming within the Municipality; (b) formally create GAD Database; and (c) prioritize measures on the development and passage of GAD Code as prescribed by the GAD law and existing regulations.

10.10	We recommended that the management (a) stop the practice of using the basic monthly compensation of regular employees as basis for the computation and remittance of the required government share to Pag-IBIG contribution; or else, excessive remittances for the ensuing period will be disallowed in Audit; and (b) make representation with Pag-IBIG for the refund of the excess contributions calculated at ₱733,579.30 for the period January to December, 2016.

13.12	We recommended that the:
Members of the SB consider providing effective measures to implement  the Traffic Ordinance to prevent undesirable outcomes as a result of unregulated proliferation of tricycles along the municipal highways, which may pose hazards and imminent danger to pedestrians and motorists’ lives and properties;
The Property Officer (i) evaluate the need to adjust the angle of CCTV installation for better visibility; (ii) tap the technical assistance of the Engineering Office personnel, (iii) consider further the evaluation of the CCTV technical specifications considering the cost effectiveness, performance efficiency and effectiveness of the CCTV for optimum utilization; and (iv) consider installing a CCTV Monitoring Unit at the TMG Office near the highway, and all other major thoroughfares, as a tool in providing immediate assistance to the public; and
Municipal Human Resource Management Officer and the Property Officer consider the proper manning of traffic personnel to determine their proper designated places and schedule of duty especially those assigned in critical or accident-prone areas and provide the necessary communication equipment, supplies and facilities for better information dissemination.

We recommended that the Local School Board and the District Supervisor (a) be guided on the proper use of SEF to ensure that only authorized priority expenditures identified under the SEF mandate are included in the SEF Annual Budget; (b) refrain from accommodating the stated non-priority expenditures which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute, otherwise the audit team will be forced to issue the corresponding Notice of Disallowance to require the restitutions of the said disbursements, and render an explanation on these charges made to SEF; and (c) stop purchasing umbrellas since it partakes personal in nature rather than for public purpose.
observations.managementAction: Management minimized spending on non- priority expenditures out of the SEF.
observations.reason:
Some non- priority expenditures were still charged out of the SEF due to lack of thorough planning and budgeting.

Reiterated under Observation No.4, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Development Council develop a systematic plan with specific schedule of implementation through priority ranking procedures in terms of urgency and impact to avoid untimely or lack of time of implementation.  Maximize utilization of the 20% Development Fund to achieve the desirable social, economic and environment development and beneficial to municipal constituents.
observations.reason: Timely implementa-tion of projects was not fully attained because of the failure to consider the period of election ban. As such, the Municipality failed to implement five identified economic development projects.

The Implementa-tion of LDRRMF was centralized to the LDRRMO during the incumbent administration.



Only 46.94% was utilized during the year. Moreover, the Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF was not submitted monthly due to non-preparation of the report on a departmental basis.

Reiterated under Observation No.8, Part II of this report.

observations.managementAction: Hiring of new sets of Casual, Contractual and Job-Order employees was done.
observations.reason:
The change in administration hindered the plan to fix organizational problems.





There remained no approved guidelines/ policy for the hiring of casual, contractual and honorarium based personnel.
















Some job order employees under the legislative department have functions which were not aligned and could be consider as under executive function. Proper classification of duties and responsibilities were not specifically stated in the contract.
Although the Municipality had complied with the preparation of a 10-year management plan and was able to implement the closure of the open dumpsite, but without a sanitary landfill thus, the commitment in the realization of goals and objectives towards a progressive, healthy and sanitary environment has not been fully attained as required under Republic Act No. 9003 otherwise known as Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Executive (a) propose to the Sangguniang Bayan to formulate policies and measures that will ensure full compliance of the barangays on the provisions of R.A. No. 9003; and (b) take appropriate action on the funding problems associated with the construction and operation of the sanitary landfill.
observations.managementAction: Management hired Barangay Eco-Aide and conducted seminars regarding Solid Waste Management.
observations.reason:
The mandatory segregation of wastes at source as to recyclable, biodegradable, and residual wastes was not actively participated in by the majority of the households, institutions, and commercial/ business establishments. 

Reiterated under Audit Observation No. 11, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management (a) institutionalize a working and functional GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) or a similar GAD mechanism to catalyze and accelerate gender mainstreaming within the Municipality; (b) formally create GFPS Committee and Technical Working Group to perform its mandated tasks; (c) cause the preparation of a comprehensive GAD Plan; and (d) prioritize measures on the development and passage of GAD Code as prescribed by the GAD law and existing regulations.
observations.managementAction: Management sent the Focal Point Person to GAD Seminars.
observations.reason:
The GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) was not fully organized due to lack of information of all the technical working group on how to catalyze and accelerate gender mainstreaming within the Municipality, including the preparation of a comprehensive GAD Plan. Passage of GAD Code was not prioritized during the  transition of new administration.

Reiterated under Observation No. 9, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management (a) enforce the terms and conditions stipulated in the contract of lease relative to the period and mode of payment for rental of market stalls, imposition of surcharge/s for late and non-payment to encourage space holders to promptly settle their accounts and avoid possible cancellation of their contract.  Strict implementation of the provisions would also benefit management from an increased collection of rental from market stalls; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to record the rental receivable from the unpaid space rentals.
observations.managementAction: Management coursed the advertisement of unoccupied stalls and conducted improvements thereon.
observations.reason:
Strict enforcement of the terms and conditions stipulated in the contract of lease could not be made due to political issue between the agency and the constituents/ stall lessee.  



Recording of rental receivables from the unpaid space rentals was not made due to lack of records from the market administrator as to the actual receivables of the Municipality from the respective stall lessee.
